title: MEGOBARI Act
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subs
plain_summary: The MEGOBARI Act is a law that punishes people who are hurting Georgia's democracy and preventing the country from joining Europe. The President must block visas and can freeze the money of Georgian government officials and other foreign people who are being corrupt or violent to stop Georgia from becoming more democratic. Family members of these bad actors can also be punished if they benefited from the wrongdoing. The law also requires reports about how China, Iran, and Russia are trying to influence Georgia. If Georgia makes progress toward democracy, they could get more help from the United States.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate. Read twice. Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 70.
subjects: Government ethics and transparency, public corruption
Presidents and presidential powers, Vice Presidents
Rule of law and government transparency
Sovereignty, recognition, national governance and status

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Mobilizing and Enhancing Georgia's Options for Building Accountability, Resilience, and Independence Act or the MEGOBARI Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, the following terms mean: Appropriate congressional committees means the Senate Committee on Foreign Relations, the Senate Committee on Appropriations, the House Committee on Foreign Affairs, and the House Committee on Appropriations. NATO means the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (a military alliance of democratic countries). Secretary means the Secretary of State (the top US diplomat).

sections.title: Section 3 Sense of Congress
sections.text: Congress believes that the people of Georgia should be praised for building an innovative and productive society since becoming independent from the Soviet Union. Making democracy stronger in Georgia is important for keeping the region stable and protecting US interests. Georgia has moved away from democracy in recent years, as shown by many independent studies. The current Georgian government is increasingly hostile toward independent civil society groups and its main European and Atlantic partners while getting closer to Russia, China, and other anti-Western authoritarian governments. The United States wants to protect and secure democracy in Georgia. The Secretary of State should suspend the United States-Georgia Strategic Partnership Commission (established in two thousand nine) until Georgia's government takes steps to represent what its citizens want democratically and fulfill its constitutional promise to work toward joining the European Union and NATO.

sections.title: Section 4 Statement of Policy
sections.text: The United States policy is to support Georgia's constitutional goal to join the European Union and NATO, which is stated in Article seventy-eight of Georgia's Constitution and supported by most Georgian citizens. The US will continue helping Georgia's government protect its independence and territory from further Russian attacks within its internationally recognized borders. The US calls on all political parties and elected members of Georgia's Parliament to keep working on the reform plan outlined by the European Commission to resume Georgia's recently granted candidate status through an inclusive and transparent process that involves opposition parties and civil society groups, which the Georgian people have freely chosen to pursue. The US will reevaluate its relationship with Georgia's government and review all foreign and security assistance if Georgia takes required steps to focus on joining the European Union and advance policies that reflect what Georgian people want. The US emphasizes the importance of contributing to international efforts to combat Russian aggression, including through trade sanctions on Russia and implementing worldwide sanctions on Russia, and to reduce rather than increase trade ties between Georgia and Russia. The US will continue supporting democratic values in Georgia, including free and fair elections, freedom of association, an independent and accountable court system, independent media, government transparency and accountability, rule of law, fighting harmful foreign influence, and anti-corruption efforts. The US will impose swift consequences on individuals directly responsible for leading actions or policies that significantly undermine these standards. The US will continue supporting the Georgian people and civil society organizations that reflect Georgian people's hopes for democracy and a future with Europe. The US will continue supporting Georgian people's right to peacefully protest, determine their future, and make independent choices on foreign and security policy, including Georgia's relationships with other countries and international organizations, without interference, intimidation, or pressure from other countries. The US calls on all political parties, elected members of Georgia's Parliament, and officers of Georgia's Ministry of Internal Affairs to respect freedoms of peaceful assembly, association, and expression, including for the press, and the rule of law, and encourage a vibrant and inclusive civil society. The US calls on Georgia's government to release all people detained or imprisoned for political reasons and drop any pending charges against them. The US calls on Georgia's government to thoroughly investigate all claims about the recent national elections that took place in October two thousand twenty-four, determine whether the elections should be considered illegitimate, and hold those responsible for election interference accountable. The US will continue impressing upon Georgia's government that the United States is committed to maintaining and deepening relations and supporting Georgia's European and Atlantic goals.

sections.title: Section 5 Reports and Briefings
sections.text: Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the Secretary of State, working with the Director of National Intelligence and Secretary of Defense, must give Congress a classified report that examines how Russian intelligence and their assets have penetrated Georgia. This report must include a section examining Chinese influence and potential Russian-Chinese cooperation in Georgia. This report will be prepared to protect intelligence sources and methods. The congressional committees that receive this report are the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, Senate Intelligence Committee, Senate Armed Services Committee, House Foreign Affairs Committee, House Intelligence Committee, and House Armed Services Committee. Within ninety days after this law is passed, the Secretary of State and the Administrator of the United States Agency for International Development, working with heads of other relevant federal departments and agencies, must give the appropriate congressional committees a detailed strategy. This strategy must outline specific goals for improving relations that reflect Georgia's current domestic political situation. It must determine what tools, resources, and funding should be available to achieve these goals and assess whether Georgia should remain the second-highest recipient of US funding in the Europe and Eurasia region. It must determine how much the United States should continue investing in its partnership with Georgia. It must include a plan for how the United States can continue supporting civil society and independent media organizations in Georgia. It must determine whether Georgia's government remains committed to expanding trade ties with the United States and Europe and whether the US government should continue investing in Georgian projects. This report must be submitted in unclassified form with a classified section.

sections.title: Section 6 Sanctions
sections.text: In this section, admission, admitted, and alien have the meanings given in section one hundred one of the Immigration and Nationality Act. Appropriate committees of Congress means the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, Senate Banking Committee, Senate Judiciary Committee, House Foreign Affairs Committee, House Judiciary Committee, and House Financial Services Committee. Foreign person means any individual or organization that is not a United States person. Immediate family members has the meaning given in section two hundred one of the Immigration and Nationality Act. Knowingly means that a person has actual knowledge or should have known about the conduct, circumstance, or result. United States person means a US citizen or someone lawfully admitted for permanent residence, an organization created under US laws including foreign branches, or any person within the United States. Within ninety days after this law is passed, the President must determine whether foreign persons have knowingly engaged in significant corruption or acts of violence or intimidation related to blocking European and Atlantic integration in Georgia. This includes any individual who has served as a member of Georgia's Parliament or as a current or former senior official of a Georgian political party since January first, two thousand fourteen. It also includes any individual serving as an official in a leadership position working for Georgia's government, including law enforcement, intelligence, judicial, or local government. It includes immediate family members of these officials who benefited from their conduct. The President must impose sanctions on each foreign person for whom the President makes an affirmative decision. Within ninety days after this law is passed, the Secretary must brief the appropriate committees of Congress about any foreign person for whom the President made an affirmative determination and the specific facts that justify each determination. The President may waive sanctions case by case if the President determines and reports to Congress that the waiver would serve national security interests or the circumstances that caused the individual to be ineligible have sufficiently changed. The President may impose asset blocking sanctions and must impose visa sanctions on each foreign person the President determines has engaged in actions intended to undermine Georgia's peace, security, stability, independence, or territorial integrity. This includes being responsible for, involved in, or directly or indirectly engaged in such actions, being a leader or official of an organization whose members engaged in such activity, or being an immediate family member who benefited from such conduct. Within ten days after imposing sanctions, the President must brief and provide written notification to Congress describing the foreign person subject to sanctions, the activity justifying sanctions, and the specific sanctions imposed. The President may waive sanctions for renewable periods not exceeding one hundred eighty days if the President submits to Congress a written determination that the waiver is in US national security interests at least fifteen days before the waiver takes effect. The sanctions include blocking all property and property interests in the United States or under control of US persons. For visa sanctions, foreign persons who are aliens will be inadmissible to the United States, ineligible to receive visas or other documentation to enter the United States, and ineligible to be admitted or receive any other benefit under immigration law. Any current visas will be revoked immediately and automatically cancel other valid visas in the person's possession. The President may use authorities under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to carry out this section. People who violate these provisions will be subject to penalties under that Act. Nothing in this Act limits the President's authority to designate or sanction people under applicable Executive orders or the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. Within one hundred twenty days after this law is passed, the President must prescribe regulations necessary to implement this section. At least ten days before prescribing regulations, the President must notify Congress of the proposed regulations. The President must determine whether foreign persons have engaged in significant corruption in Georgia or acts intended to undermine Georgia's peace, security, stability, independence, or territorial integrity for potential sanctions under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. Within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the President must submit a report to Congress identifying all foreign persons determined to have engaged in such activities, the dates sanctions were imposed, and reasons for imposing sanctions. This report will be unclassified but may include a classified section. Sanctions on a foreign person will end when the President certifies to Congress that the person is no longer engaging in activities that led to sanctions or on the sunset date in section seven. Sanctions do not apply to intelligence activities subject to reporting requirements under the National Security Act or authorized US intelligence activities. Sanctions do not apply if admitting the person is necessary for the United States to comply with the UN Headquarters Agreement or other international obligations. Sanctions do not apply to transactions for providing agricultural commodities, food, medicine, medical devices, or humanitarian assistance, or transactions necessary for such activities. The requirement to block property does not include authority to impose sanctions on importing goods.

sections.title: Section 7 Additional Assistance With Respect to Georgia
sections.text: After the President submits the certification described in this section to Congress, the Secretary of State should work with other relevant federal departments and agencies to enhance people-to-people contacts and academic exchanges between the United States and Georgia. The President should work with the Secretary of Defense to maintain and expand military cooperation with Georgia, including providing security and defense equipment suited for territorial defense against Russian aggression and related training, maintenance, and operations support. Congress believes that after the certification is submitted, if Georgia's government takes steps to realign itself with its European and Atlantic agenda, including significant changes to the foreign influence law, the President should take steps to improve relations between the United States and Georgia, including actions to strengthen Georgia's ability to deter threats from Russia and other harmful actors. The certification described in this section is a certification submitted to Congress by the President that Georgia has shown significant and sustained progress toward reinvigorating its democracy and advancing its European and Atlantic integration.

sections.title: Section 8 Sunset
sections.text: This law will stop having any effect beginning five years after the date it is passed.
